output_text:
Tightened the draft for flow, sharpened the contribution statement, and softened one overreaching claim; flagged a few items the author should verify.

---

**Revised draft:**

Onboard state-of-health (SOH) estimation for lithium-ion battery packs is a central challenge for vehicle battery management systems (BMS). Direct measurement of capacity fade requires full discharge cycles that are incompatible with normal vehicle operation, motivating indirect estimation from the voltage, current, and temperature signals available during partial duty cycles. The literature is dominated by two methodological streams: equivalent-circuit models (ECMs) with adaptive parameter tracking (Plett, 2004; Hu et al., 2012), and data-driven analyses of incremental capacity (ICA) and differential voltage (DVA) curves (Dubarry et al., 2012; Weng et al., 2014).

Hybrid frameworks have recently attempted to combine the two. The Joint Physics-Informed Capacity Estimator (JPICE) of Tanaka and Reinhardt (2020) is representative: an electrochemical pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model supplies regularization gradients to a recurrent network trained on ICA peak features, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) of 1.4% on the NASA PCoE aging dataset. JPICE has become a common reference point for partial-cycle hybrid SOH estimation, but its P2D regularizer is too expensive for deployment on automotive-grade microcontrollers.

This paper makes two contributions. First, we replace the P2D regularizer with a single-particle model with electrolyte dynamics (SPMe), cutting per-step inference cost from 11.2 ms to 3.6 ms on an NXP S32K344 — a roughly 3× speedup — with no loss of physical consistency on the regularization path. Second, we introduce a temperature-aware peak-tracking loss that penalizes inconsistencies between ICA peaks observed at different cell temperatures within the same partial cycle, exploiting a supervision signal that prior hybrid approaches discard.

On the Oxford Battery Degradation Dataset, our method achieves an MAE of 1.1% across cells aged from 100% to 70% of nominal capacity — a 21% relative improvement over JPICE at roughly one-third the inference cost. Together, these results offer a practical pathway to deploying physics-informed SOH estimation on resource-constrained automotive BMS hardware.

---

**Key changes:**
- Reframed paragraph 3 as an explicit "two contributions" statement and added the *why* of each change (deployability; previously discarded supervision signal), so the contributions read as motivated rather than as a list of tweaks.
- Softened "now widely regarded as the canonical hybrid baseline" to "a common reference point" — the original phrasing is the kind of claim reviewers ask you to cite or remove.
- Added a bridge sentence at the end of paragraph 2 ("but its P2D regularizer is too expensive…") so paragraph 3 follows from a stated gap rather than appearing as an arbitrary extension.
- Tightened wording throughout (e.g., "Direct measurement … requires full discharge cycles that are incompatible with normal vehicle operation" reads more cleanly; removed redundant "captured during normal duty cycles").
- Quantified the speedup inline (3×) since reviewers tend to want the headline number stated, not computed.

**Uncertainty disclosure:** I did not verify the existence of Tanaka and Reinhardt (2020) / "JPICE," the cited MAE of 1.4% on NASA PCoE, the 11.2 ms / 3.6 ms timings on the NXP S32K344, or the 1.1% MAE on the Oxford dataset. The arithmetic on the claimed gains is internally consistent (1.4 → 1.1 is ~21%; 3.6/11.2 ≈ 0.32), but the underlying numbers and the JPICE citation should be confirmed against your sources before submission — "JPICE" in particular does not match a paper I can corroborate.
